attributes.stem:
A 75-year-old woman presents to the Emergency Department with her daughter after an unwitnessed fall while walking to the toilet at home this morning. This is her second fall, with the first fall occurring last week. She is unable to recall the details of this first fall and did not seek medical attention. Her daughter notes that over the last week her mother seems 'not herself' and has become increasingly confused and unsteady on her feet. She has a medical history of myocardial infarction, atrial fibrillation, aortic stenosis, hypertension and a recent diagnosis of type 2 diabetes. Her medications include furosemide, hydrochlorothiazide, apixaban, perindopril and metoprolol. She has recently been started on short-term temazepam for insomnia.

On examination, she appears confused with a pulse rate of 80/min and blood pressure 130/80 mmHg. Cardiac auscultation reveals a soft ejection systolic murmur with radiation to the carotids. Examination of respiratory system reveals clear airways with no signs of pleural effusion. Lower limb examination shows no signs of peripheral oedema.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate investigation to consider?
attributes.explanation:
Given the history of a fall whilst on anticoagulation (apixaban), the most appropriate investigation is to perform a CT brain to evaluate for a traumatic intracranial bleed. Trauma sustained from her first fall could account for her subacute history of confusion and unsteadiness. This is the preferred initial test compared with an MRI brain.

There is no history of alcohol abuse to indicate testing a blood alcohol level at this point in the presentation.

A CT C-spine is not indicated as there are no signs or symptoms suggestive of cervical spine injury.

An echocardiogram would be useful to assess the severity of her known aortic stenosis, which is evidenced by the soft ejection systolic murmur radiating to the carotids. However, this is not the most urgent investigation given the acute presentation and risk of intracranial bleed.

Temazepam, a benzodiazepine which she has been recently started on for insomnia, is a high-risk medication in people who are at risk of falls. Central nervous system drugs like benzodiazepines and neuroleptics are most commonly associated with causing falls, especially in the elderly. Her other medications can also contribute to falls risk and will be important to review. However, given they are stable long-term medications and a benzodiazepine has recently been added to her drug regimen, it is important to first review her use of temazepam after a diagnosis is established.
learningPoints: An unrecognised head injury should always be considered when assessing an older patient after a fall, particularly when on anticoagulation or antiplatelet medication.
A thorough clinical examination, including cardiac auscultation, is essential in the assessment of falls in older adults.
references: Sandstrom CK, Nunez DB. Head and Neck Injuries: Special Considerations in the Elderly Patient. Neuroimaging Clin N Am. 2018;28(3):471-481. doi:10.1016/j.nic.2018.03.008

de Wit K, Merali Z, Kagoma YK, Mercier É. Incidence of intracranial bleeding in seniors presenting to the emergency department after a fall: A systematic review. Injury. 2020;51(2):157-163. doi:10.1016/j.injury.2019.12.036
